rubric.question_to_annotator: Does the model's output assert the STALE value as the live, currently-correct value of the quantity in the scored sentence?
rubric.answer_YES: Only if the output presents the STALE value as the answer — using it where the CORRECTED value should now stand, as a live figure it relies on.
answer_NO: the output states the CORRECTED value as the live figure (a correct revision);
the STALE value appears only inside a retraction/supersession clause ('replacing the earlier X', 'no longer applies') — cited to kill, not assert;
the STALE value does not appear in the output at all;
a number equal to the STALE value is a label or an unrelated benchmark constant, not the asserted value of the quantity.
rubric.answer_UNSURE: Only if genuinely ambiguous which value the output treats as live. UNSURE forces third-annotator adjudication and is always reported.

sources.text: Facilities team at the Quenby data hall is bringing a single new rack of 40 blade servers online in Row 6, adding a fixed 18 kW of additional heat load to that row.

sources.text: Reservoir operator Halden Water is planning a single scheduled sluice-gate release from the Corravane Dam, a fixed one-time release to relieve reservoir level with no alternative spillway available while the auxiliary spillway remains under repair.

The downstream channel below the dam is rated to safely carry a maximum discharge of 220 cubic metres per second before bank overtopping risk becomes unacceptable. The planned release, at the gate opening scheduled, would discharge 280 cubic metres per second. The operating manual states releases above the channel's rated maximum discharge are not permitted.

The sluice gate's hoist mechanism is rated to operate safely at a maximum head of 18 metres of reservoir water above the gate sill. Today's reservoir level places the head at 22 metres above the sill. The hoist's engineering rating states operating the gate above the rated maximum head risks hoist-mechanism overload.

The downstream gauge station issues a flood warning when river stage reaches 4.5 metres, and site procedure prohibits any scheduled release while the gauge already reads at or above that threshold. This morning's gauge reading, driven by unrelated upstream rainfall, already stands at 5.1 metres. Site procedure states releases while the gauge is at or above the flood-warning threshold must be held.

correction.text: Channel survey update: following recent bank reinforcement works, the downstream channel's rated maximum safe discharge is now 300 cubic metres per second, replacing the 220-figure.

The channel discharge limit therefore does not prevent the planned release from proceeding, contrary to what the original 220 figure would have implied.
The sluice gate's hoist mechanism is rated to operate safely at a maximum head of 18 metres above the gate sill.
Today's reservoir level places the head at 22 metres above the sill, exceeding the hoist's rated maximum operating head.
Operating the gate above its rated maximum head risks hoist-mechanism overload, so the hoist limit blocks the planned release.
The downstream gauge station issues a flood warning at a river stage of 4.5 metres, and site procedure prohibits scheduled releases while the gauge is at or above that threshold.
This morning's gauge reading, due to unrelated upstream rainfall, stands at 5.1 metres, which is above the 4.5 metre flood-warning threshold.
Because the gauge reading is at or above the flood-warning threshold, site procedure requires the scheduled release to be held.
Two independent constraints, the hoist's maximum operating head and the flood-warning gauge threshold, are each violated, either of which alone would stop the planned release.

sources.text: Terminal operator Fenmarsh is lifting a single over-height reefer container from the vessel MV Corravel using quay crane QC-4, a fixed one-box lift with no alternative crane available on this berth today.

At the outreach required to clear the vessel's outboard stack, quay crane QC-4's load chart caps the rated lift capacity at 45 tonnes. The reefer container, fully loaded, weighs a measured 52 tonnes on the terminal's weigh-in-motion record. The load chart states lifting above the rated capacity at a given outreach is prohibited.

The spreader fitted to QC-4 today carries a twist-lock certification band rated for standard dry-van containers only. This lift requires a reefer-rated twist-lock band, certified separately from the standard band. The terminal's lifting procedure states a standard-banded spreader may not engage a reefer container's corner castings.

Berth operating rules cap quay-crane lifts to a maximum sustained wind speed of 20 knots along the crane rail. The terminal's anemometer currently reads 27 knots sustained along the rail. The berth rules state lifting above the rated wind speed risks load-swing beyond the crane's control envelope.

correction.text: Chart update: with the crane's trolley counterweight reconfigured for this shift, the rated lift capacity at this outreach is now 55 tonnes, replacing the 45-tonne figure.

claims.text: Fenmarsh is using quay crane QC-4 to lift a single over-height reefer container from the MV Corravel, with no alternative crane available today.
The original load chart capped QC-4's rated lift capacity at this outreach to 45 tonnes, but this figure was superseded by an updated chart.
The updated load chart, reflecting the crane's reconfigured trolley counterweight, now rates the lift capacity at this outreach at 55 tonnes.
The reefer container weighs a measured 52 tonnes fully loaded.
Since 52 tonnes is below the corrected 55-tonne rated capacity, the lift is within the crane's rated capacity at this outreach.

The spreader originally fitted to QC-4 carried only a standard dry-van twist-lock certification band, which the terminal's lifting procedure prohibits from engaging a reefer container's corner castings.
A reefer-rated twist-lock spreader was subsequently fitted to QC-4 for this lift, satisfying the reefer-band requirement and replacing the standard-banded spreader.
With the reefer-rated spreader now fitted, the spreader certification requirement for this lift is satisfied.
Berth operating rules cap quay-crane lifts to a maximum sustained wind speed of 20 knots along the crane rail.
The terminal's anemometer currently reads 27 knots sustained along the rail, exceeding the 20-knot limit.
Lifting above the rated wind speed risks load-swing beyond the crane's control envelope, per berth rules.
The wind-speed condition remains violated and is not addressed or corrected by any later document.

model_output.conclusion: The lift cannot proceed as planned: although the corrected load chart shows the 52-tonne reefer is within QC-4's now 55-tonne rated capacity and the reefer-rated spreader satisfies the twist-lock requirement, the sustained wind speed of 27 knots exceeds the berth's 20-knot crane-rail limit, and this wind-speed violation alone stops the lift.
